pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi frames the practitioner transition decision as a short-term versus permanent tradeoff: transitioning clients causes short-term revenue loss, but failing to transition means the founder is permanently the delivery bottleneck. The asymmetry favors transitioning.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that switching an entire business model is the worst-case option and should be treated as a last resort (the Armageddon button), because there are almost always intermediate fixes—such as finding better-covered customers or improving the sales close process—that address the symptom without destroying the existing revenue engine.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that for service businesses with a demand constraint (not a supply constraint), the correct sequencing is: fix the sales motion first, increase prices second, and use the resulting profit increase to raise team compensation third — because attempting to raise prices before fixing the sales process will collapse an already-low close rate into an unworkable range.

pairs.b_topic: The Execution Gap: Knowledge Without Action
pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that serially jumping from opportunity to opportunity at the first sign of difficulty is the primary reason entrepreneurs fail to achieve large outcomes: each jump restarts the cycle at uninformed optimism, meaning the entrepreneur never passes through informed pessimism and the valley of despair to reach the upside on any single path.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the primary reason entrepreneurs know what to do but do not act is an inability to delay gratification: humans are evolutionarily conditioned to repeat rewarded behaviors, so the current equilibrium (talking about goals, maintaining the status quo) delivers more immediate reward than the uncertain, painful path of change.

pairs.b_topic: The Execution Gap: Knowledge Without Action
pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the hardest part of any pursuit is not the beginning (getting started) but continuing to do unglamorous repetitive work once the initial excitement fades and rewards are not guaranteed. He asserts that inability to work without reward for extended periods limits potential far more than lack of talent.

pairs.a_topic: The Execution Gap: Knowledge Without Action
pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the specificity of instruction required is inversely correlated with the skill level of the recipient: a low-skill person needs every sub-step explained (e.g., what a browser is, how to type), while a high-skill person can execute from a single high-level directive. Entrepreneurs misidentify this as a motivation problem when it is actually a skill-gap problem.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi states that skills are chains of adapted behaviors: multiple instances of same-condition/new-behavior each linked in sequence to produce a composite outcome. A macro-level skill label (business, basketball) is a bucket containing many sub-skills, each of which must be deconstructed to individual observable behaviors before learning can occur.
